participants.extracted: Community-dwelling Black American adults aged 30 to 65 years with metabolic syndrome risk factors. Eligible participants had either standard ATP III metabolic syndrome (at least 3 of 5 criteria: abdominal obesity, elevated triglycerides, low HDL-cholesterol, elevated blood pressure, and elevated fasting glucose) or a modified definition requiring 2 of 3 criteria (abdominal obesity, elevated blood pressure, elevated fasting glucose) because standard criteria may underestimate risk in Black Americans. Participants were excluded if they had known cardiovascular disease or renovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, were current smokers, had recent use of vitamins A/C/E, were pregnant, or had alcohol/drug abuse history or other conditions that would interfere with one year of attendance.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was a sound- or mantra-based meditation program called consciously resting meditation (CRM), modeled on transcendental meditation. It was delivered over 12 months in 21 sessions by the same experienced teacher in group format, beginning with four consecutive daily instruction sessions followed by 17 follow-up sessions. Sessions lasted about 1 to 1.5 hours and typically included group meditation plus a lecture, discussion, or videotape. Participants were instructed to practice CRM twice daily for 20 minutes and were given the same health education reading materials as the control group. The control intervention was health education (HE) matched for number, size, and frequency of meetings and delivered by a professional health educator. HE covered cardiovascular disease prevention through lifestyle modification, including healthy diet, exercise, weight management, and the role of stress in these behaviors, but did not include any stress reduction or relaxation techniques. HE participants were also instructed to do 20-minute twice-daily home practice based on diet, exercise, or other lifestyle habits.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure primary outcome endothelium-dependent flow-mediated vasodilation (FMD) of the brachial artery by ultrasound, assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary vascular outcomes included endothelium-independent nitroglycerin-mediated vasodilation (NMD) and arterial stiffness, measured using pulse wave velocity (PWV) and augmentation index (AI), at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary psychosocial and behavioral outcomes included perceived stress (Cohen Perceived Stress Scale), hostility (Cook-Medley Hostility Inventory), anger (Spielberger Anger Expression Inventory), anxiety (Spielberger State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, including state and trait subscales), depressive symptoms (Beck Depression Inventory II, including cognitive/affective and somatic subscales), and physical activity (CAPS Typical Week Physical Activity Survey, focusing on moderate and vigorous activity), assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Additional secondary metabolic outcomes included abdominal obesity/waist circumference, body weight, body mass index, systolic and diastolic blood pressure, fasting plasma glucose, triglycerides, HDL-cholesterol, and a composite count of metabolic syndrome risk factors, measured at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Biomarker outcomes included leptin, adiponectin, C-reactive protein (CRP), interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α), and plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1), assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months.
